1:
"And I saw another sign in heaven, great and marvellous, seven angels having the seven last plagues; for in them is filled up the wrath of God." The witnesses note the seven angels with the seven last plagues. On "another sign in heaven, great and marvellous, seven angels having the seven last plagues," the grammatical voices note the new vision. Barnes and Robertson note the introduction: "another sign in heaven, great and marvellous, seven angels having the seven last plagues" — a great and marvelous sign appears: seven angels bearing the seven last plagues; these introduce the final series of judgments (the seven vials, ch. 16); "great and marvellous" marks the importance and awe of the vision.

On "the seven last plagues," the witnesses note the finality. Barnes and Ellicott note that these are "the seven last plagues" — the final series of judgments, completing God's wrath; "last" because with them the outpouring of wrath is finished; the climax of the judgments on the antichristian world.

On "for in them is filled up the wrath of God," the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede and the grammatical-historical note the completion of wrath: "in them is filled up (completed) the wrath of God" — in these plagues the wrath of God is brought to its full and final expression; the measure of God's judgment is completed; the seven last plagues consummate the divine wrath against the impenitent. The verse displays the seven angels with the seven last plagues.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the vision — "another sign in heaven, great and marvellous, seven angels having the seven last plagues," the seven angels introducing the final judgments (Barnes, Robertson); "the seven last plagues" the final series completing God's wrath (Barnes, Ellicott); "in them is filled up the wrath of God," the wrath brought to its full and final expression (Victorinus, Bede, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the final plagues. The verse marks the sign: great and marvellous. The verse displays the angels: seven. The verse displays the plagues: the last. The verse displays the wrath: filled up. The vision displays the final judgments completing God's wrath. The verse displays the sign: seen. The verse displays the plagues: seven. The verse displays the wrath: completed. The seven angels with the seven last plagues display the opening of the final judgments: a great and marvelous sign — seven angels bearing the seven last plagues, in which the wrath of God is filled up and completed. The verse displays the seven plagues, after the song of the victors (vv.2-4).
2:
"And I saw as it were a sea of glass mingled with fire: and them that had gotten the victory over the beast, and over his image, and over his mark, and over the number of his name, stand on the sea of glass, having the harps of God." The witnesses note the victors over the beast standing on the sea of glass. On "a sea of glass mingled with fire," the grammatical voices note the vision before the throne. Barnes and Robertson note the scene: "a sea of glass mingled with fire" — the sea of glass (4:6, before the throne) now mingled with fire; the fire marking the judgments, or the trials through which the victors passed, or the glory and holiness of God; the calm, crystalline sea now shot with fire.

On "them that had gotten the victory over the beast, and over his image, and over his mark, and over the number of his name," the witnesses note the victors. Barnes and Ellicott note that those who "had gotten the victory over the beast" — overcame the beast, his image, mark, and the number of his name (ch. 13) by refusing to worship and bear the mark, even unto death — stand on the sea of glass; the faithful who conquered the antichristian power are seen triumphant.

On "having the harps of God," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note their worship: the victors stand "having the harps of God" — given harps for the song of triumph (vv.3-4); the conquerors, like the redeemed (14:2-3; 5:8), praise God with harps; the victory over the beast issues in heavenly worship and song. The verse displays the victors over the beast standing on the sea of glass.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the scene — "a sea of glass mingled with fire," the crystal sea before the throne now shot with fire of judgment/trial (4:6, Barnes, Robertson); "them that had gotten the victory over the beast... his image... mark... number," the faithful who overcame the antichristian power (ch. 13, Barnes, Ellicott); "having the harps of God," the victors given harps for the song of triumph (5:8, Cyril). The verse displays the triumphant victors. The verse marks the sea: of glass and fire. The verse displays the victors: over the beast. The verse displays the victory: image, mark, number. The verse displays the harps: of God. The vision displays the conquerors of the beast worshiping in triumph. The verse displays the sea: glassy, fiery. The verse displays the victors: standing. The verse displays the harps: held. The victors over the beast standing on the sea of glass display the triumph of the faithful: those who overcame the beast, his image, mark, and number stand on the sea of glass mingled with fire, holding the harps of God — the conquerors of the antichristian power, victorious and ready to sing. The verse displays the harping victors, singing the song of Moses and the Lamb (vv.3-4).
3:
"And they sing the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb, saying, Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty; just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints." The witnesses note the song of Moses and the Lamb. On "they sing the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb," the grammatical voices note the song of triumph. Barnes and Robertson note the worship: "they sing the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb" — the victors sing a song joining "the song of Moses" (the song of deliverance at the Red Sea, Exodus 15, after God overthrew Pharaoh) and "the song of the Lamb" (the song of redemption); the deliverance from the beast is celebrated as a new and greater Exodus; the two songs are one — God's triumph over his enemies and the redemption of his people.

On "Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty," the witnesses note the praise of God's works. Barnes and Ellicott note the song's content: "Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty" — the victors praise God's great and marvelous works (his mighty acts of judgment and salvation); the song magnifies the Almighty for his deeds.

On "just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints," the grammatical-historical note the praise of God's justice: "just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints (or of the nations/ages)" — God's ways in judgment and salvation are just and true; the victors vindicate God's righteousness; even the dreadful judgments are owned as just and true; God is praised as King. The verse displays the song of Moses and the Lamb.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the song — "the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb," the victors' song joining the Exodus deliverance and the Lamb's redemption (Exodus 15, Barnes, Robertson); "Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty," the praise of God's mighty works (Barnes, Ellicott); "just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints," the vindication of God's just and true ways (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the song of triumph. The verse marks the song: of Moses and the Lamb. The verse displays the works: great and marvellous. The verse displays the ways: just and true. The verse displays the King: of saints. The song displays the victors praising God's mighty, righteous acts. The verse displays the song: sung. The verse displays the works: magnified. The verse displays the ways: vindicated. The song of Moses and the Lamb displays the worship of the victors: they sing the song of Moses and of the Lamb — "Great and marvellous are thy works, Lord God Almighty; just and true are thy ways, thou King of saints" — celebrating the new Exodus deliverance from the beast and vindicating God's just and true judgments. The verse displays the song, continuing in praise of God's holiness (v.4).
4:
"Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name? for thou only art holy: for all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest." The brief verse notes the song's call to fear and worship the holy God. The grammatical voices note the rhetorical praise. Barnes and Robertson note the continuation of the song: "Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name?" — the victors' song asks who would not fear and glorify God; the rhetorical question affirms that all ought to, and ultimately will, fear and honor him; the universal homage due to God.

On "for thou only art holy," the witnesses note God's holiness. Barnes and Ellicott note the ground: "for thou only art holy" — God alone is holy (the supreme reason for fearing and glorifying him); his unique holiness demands universal reverence; the song magnifies God's holiness as the ground of worship.

On "all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest," the grammatical-historical note the universal worship and the manifest judgments: "all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest" — the song foresees all nations coming to worship God (the universal acknowledgment of his reign, Psalm 86:9); the reason given is that "thy judgments are made manifest" — God's righteous judgments, now revealed, vindicate him and draw the nations to worship; the manifestation of his justice leads to universal homage. The verse displays the song's call to fear and worship the holy God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the praise — "Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name?" the rhetorical affirmation that all ought to fear and glorify God (Barnes, Robertson); "for thou only art holy," God's unique holiness the ground of worship (Barnes, Ellicott); "all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest," the universal worship drawn by God's manifest righteous judgments (Psalm 86:9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the call to worship. The verse marks the fear: who shall not. The verse displays the holiness: thou only art holy. The verse displays the nations: shall worship. The verse displays the judgments: made manifest. The song displays all called to fear and worship the holy God. The verse displays the fear: due. The verse displays the holiness: God's. The verse displays the worship: universal. The song's call to fear and worship the holy God displays the close of the victors' song: who shall not fear and glorify God, for he alone is holy, and all nations shall come and worship before him, for his righteous judgments are made manifest — the universal homage due to the holy God whose justice is revealed. The verse displays the worshiped God, as the temple of judgment is opened (vv.5-6).
5:
"And after that I looked, and, behold, the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened:" The brief verse notes the opening of the heavenly temple of the testimony. The grammatical voices note the opened sanctuary. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened" — the heavenly temple, called "the tabernacle of the testimony" (after the earthly tabernacle that held the ark of the testimony, Exodus 38:21; Numbers 1:50), is opened; the source of the coming judgments is the very sanctuary of God; the plagues proceed from God's holy presence.

On "the tabernacle of the testimony," the witnesses note the name. Barnes and Henry note that "the tabernacle of the testimony" recalls the earthly tabernacle containing the testimony (the law, the ark) — the dwelling of God's holiness and covenant; the heavenly temple so named marks that the judgments proceed from God's holiness and faithfulness to his covenant and law.

On the opening, the puritan Henry notes the significance: the opening of the temple of the testimony shows that the coming judgments are from God himself, out of his sanctuary; they are not arbitrary but proceed from his holiness and the vindication of his testimony; the plagues are God's own appointed acts. The verse displays the opening of the heavenly temple of the testimony.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the opening — "the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened," the heavenly sanctuary opened, the source of the coming judgments (Exodus 38:21, Barnes, Robertson); "the tabernacle of the testimony" recalling the dwelling of God's holiness and covenant (Barnes, Henry); the opening showing the judgments proceed from God's holiness (Henry). The verse displays the opened temple. The verse marks the temple: of the testimony. The verse displays the opening: in heaven. The verse displays the source: God's sanctuary. The verse displays the judgments: from his holiness. The vision displays the plagues proceeding from God's holy presence. The verse displays the temple: opened. The verse displays the testimony: God's. The verse displays the source: heavenly. The opening of the heavenly temple of the testimony displays the divine source of the final judgments: the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven is opened — the coming plagues proceed from God's own sanctuary, from his holiness and the vindication of his testimony. The verse displays the opened temple, from which the angels come (v.6).
6:
"And the seven angels came out of the temple, having the seven plagues, clothed in pure and white linen, and having their breasts girded with golden girdles." The brief verse notes the seven angels emerging in priestly array. The grammatical voices note the angels and their dress. Barnes and Robertson note the procession: "the seven angels came out of the temple, having the seven plagues, clothed in pure and white linen, and having their breasts girded with golden girdles" — the seven angels with the plagues come out of the opened temple, clothed in pure white linen and girded with golden girdles; the priestly, glorious array marks their holy office and the sacredness of their commission.

On "clothed in pure and white linen," the witnesses note the purity. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "pure and white linen" marks the holiness, purity, and glory of the angels; the white linen is the dress of priestly, holy service; the agents of God's judgment are themselves holy.

On "their breasts girded with golden girdles," the grammatical-historical note the golden girdles: the angels are "girded with golden girdles" (like the glorified Christ, 1:13) — the golden girdle marks dignity, royalty, and priestly office; the angels of the plagues are arrayed as holy, dignified ministers of God's judgment, proceeding from his sanctuary on their solemn errand. The verse displays the seven angels emerging in priestly array.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the procession — "the seven angels came out of the temple, having the seven plagues, clothed in pure and white linen," the angels emerging in holy, priestly array (Barnes, Robertson); "pure and white linen" the holiness and glory of the angels (Barnes, Ellicott); "their breasts girded with golden girdles," the dignity and priestly office, like the glorified Christ (1:13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the arrayed angels. The verse marks the angels: from the temple. The verse displays the plagues: borne. The verse displays the linen: pure and white. The verse displays the girdles: golden. The procession displays the holy ministers of judgment arrayed in glory. The verse displays the angels: emerging. The verse displays the linen: white. The verse displays the girdles: golden. The seven angels emerging in priestly array display the agents of the final judgments: the seven angels with the plagues come out of the temple, clothed in pure white linen and girded with golden girdles — the holy, dignified ministers of God's judgment, proceeding from his sanctuary on their solemn errand. The verse displays the arrayed angels, given the vials of wrath (v.7).
7:
"And one of the four beasts gave unto the seven angels seven golden vials full of the wrath of God, who liveth for ever and ever." The brief verse notes the golden vials of wrath given to the angels. The grammatical voices note the handing of the vials. Barnes and Robertson note the act: "one of the four beasts (living creatures) gave unto the seven angels seven golden vials (bowls) full of the wrath of God, who liveth for ever and ever" — one of the four living creatures (4:6) gives the seven angels seven golden bowls filled with God's wrath; the vials, the vessels of the plagues, are handed to the angels for the outpouring; the wrath is that of the eternal, living God.

On "seven golden vials full of the wrath of God," the witnesses note the vessels of wrath. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "golden vials (bowls)" full of the wrath of God are the vessels from which the seven last plagues will be poured out (ch. 16); golden, marking their sacred origin; full of wrath, ready to be emptied on the earth; the instruments of the final judgment.

On "who liveth for ever and ever," the grammatical-historical note the eternal God: the wrath is that of God "who liveth for ever and ever" — the everliving, eternal God, whose wrath is therefore sure and inescapable; the judgments proceed from the eternal sovereign; the appeal to God's eternity marks the certainty and gravity of the wrath. The verse displays the golden vials of wrath given to the angels.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the act — "one of the four beasts gave unto the seven angels seven golden vials full of the wrath of God," the living creature handing the angels the bowls of wrath (4:6, Barnes, Robertson); "seven golden vials full of the wrath of God" the vessels of the final plagues, ready to be poured out (ch. 16, Barnes, Ellicott); "who liveth for ever and ever," the wrath of the eternal God, sure and inescapable (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vials of wrath. The verse marks the giver: a living creature. The verse displays the vials: seven, golden. The verse displays the contents: God's wrath. The verse displays the God: everliving. The act displays the bowls of wrath readied for the angels. The verse displays the vials: given. The verse displays the wrath: full. The verse displays the God: eternal. The golden vials of wrath given to the angels display the readying of the final plagues: one of the living creatures gives the seven angels seven golden bowls full of the wrath of the eternal God — the vessels of the seven last plagues, ready to be poured out. The verse displays the vials of wrath, filling the temple with smoke (v.8).
8:
"And the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God, and from his power; and no man was able to enter into the temple, till the seven plagues of the seven angels were fulfilled." The witnesses note the temple filled with smoke until the plagues are fulfilled. On "the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God, and from his power," the grammatical voices note the cloud of glory. Barnes and Robertson note the manifestation: "the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God, and from his power" — the heavenly temple is filled with smoke proceeding from God's glory and power; the smoke (like the cloud that filled the tabernacle and Solomon's temple, Exodus 40:34-35; 1 Kings 8:10-11; Isaiah 6:4) marks the immediate, overwhelming presence and majesty of God.

On "smoke from the glory of God, and from his power," the witnesses note the divine majesty. Barnes and Ellicott note that the smoke arises "from the glory of God, and from his power" — the visible token of God's awful presence, glory, and might; the manifestation of God in his holiness and power as the judgments are about to be poured out.

On "no man was able to enter into the temple, till the seven plagues... were fulfilled," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the closed sanctuary: "no man was able to enter into the temple, till the seven plagues of the seven angels were fulfilled" — none could enter the temple while the judgments proceeded; the closed sanctuary marks that the time of intercession is, for the moment, suspended — the judgments must run their course; God's awful presence in judgment bars approach until the wrath is fulfilled; the solemnity and finality of the appointed plagues. The verse displays the temple filled with smoke until the plagues are fulfilled.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cloud — "the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God, and from his power," the smoke marking God's overwhelming presence and majesty (Exodus 40:34; 1 Kings 8:10-11; Isaiah 6:4, Barnes, Robertson); "from the glory of God, and from his power," the token of his awful presence and might (Barnes, Ellicott); "no man was able to enter... till the seven plagues... were fulfilled," the closed sanctuary, the judgments running their course until fulfilled (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the smoke-filled temple. The verse marks the smoke: from God's glory. The verse displays the presence: overwhelming. The verse displays the temple: barred. The verse displays the time: till the plagues fulfilled. The vision displays God's awful presence in judgment barring approach. The verse displays the temple: filled. The verse displays the glory: manifest. The verse displays the plagues: to be fulfilled. The temple filled with smoke until the plagues are fulfilled displays the awful close of the chapter: the temple is filled with smoke from the glory and power of God, and none can enter until the seven plagues are fulfilled — God's overwhelming presence in judgment, the appointed wrath running its course before approach is possible. The verse displays the smoke-filled temple, opening the outpouring of the seven vials (ch.16).
